exercises.title: A: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Read the text. Some lines contain one extra word that should not be there. Click the extra word. If a line is correct, leave it as it is.
exercises.studentInstruction: Some lines contain one extra word. Tap the extra word. If a line is correct, tap OK.

items.sentence: Last week, I entered a [few] local radio station competition asking if
items.explanation: **Few** is unnecessary — say **a local radio station competition** (not "a few local").

items.sentence: they were giving away [the] free passes to a recording session. Two days later,
items.explanation: **The** is wrong before **free passes** — remove it.

items.sentence: they emailed [to] me two tickets to attend a live broadcast. I went along with
my friend Carlos last Friday. It was [an] amazing! The programme
items.explanation: **Amazing** is an adjective, not a noun — remove **an** (say "It was amazing!").

items.sentence: is called Spin the Wheel and listeners phone in to try to
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: win prizes by answering [a] lots of questions about music and culture,
items.explanation: **Lots of** already means many — **a** is the extra word.

items.sentence: like song titles, artists and album names. There are five rounds
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: in total and contestants get eliminated one by one as the show
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: goes on. I'd love to go [into] on a competition like that myself
items.explanation: **Go on** a competition — **into** is the extra word (not "go into on").

items.sentence: but, for now, I'm [much] happy just to watch from the audience.
items.explanation: **Happy** needs no intensifier here — **much** is the extra word.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: Why can't (POLITICS) ...... ever give clear answers about policy issues?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is people in politics, so the correct answer is "politicians".

wrongOptions.politics: The root POLITICS cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "politicians".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of POLITICS that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: POLITICS → politicians: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "politicians" is the word-formation answer from the root POLITICS in this context.

items.sentence: I hoped this documentary would help my project, but it wasn't very (INFORM) .......

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is useful for learning, so the correct answer is "informative".

wrongOptions.inform: The root INFORM cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "informative".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of INFORM that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: INFORM → informative: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "informative" is the word-formation answer from the root INFORM in this context.

items.sentence: My older brother has kept a personal (JOURNAL) ...... since he was ten years old.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is personal diary, so the correct answer is "journal".

wrongOptions.journal: The root JOURNAL cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "journal".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of JOURNAL that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: JOURNAL → journal: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "journal" is the word-formation answer from the root JOURNAL in this context.

items.sentence: Did you hear that (ANNOUNCE) ...... on the radio about the road closure?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is something heard on radio, so the correct answer is "announcement".

wrongOptions.announce: The root ANNOUNCE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "announcement".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ANNOUNCE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ANNOUNCE → announcement: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "announcement" is the word-formation answer from the root ANNOUNCE in this context.

items.sentence: There seems to be an (WRITE) ...... rule that celebrities must always smile for the cameras.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not written down, so the correct answer is "unwritten".

wrongOptions.write: The root WRITE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "unwritten".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of WRITE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: WRITE → unwritten: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "unwritten" is the word-formation answer from the root WRITE in this context.

items.sentence: I stood there in complete (BELIEVE) ...... when I heard my name called out on the show.

wrongOptions.believe: The root BELIEVE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "disbelief".

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "disbelief" is the word-formation answer from the root BELIEVE in this context.

wrongOptions.communicate: The root COMMUNICATE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "communication".

explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

wrongOptions.humour: The root HUMOUR cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "humorous".

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
These two candidates are very similar. **little**
There ...... these two candidates.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very similar, so the correct answer is "is little difference between".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is little difference between", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Her drama teacher greatly influenced her. **influence**
Her drama teacher ...... her.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is strong effect on someone, so the correct answer is "had a great influence on".

wrongOptions.a great influence on: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had a great influence on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
She described the accident to the police reporter. **gave**
She ...... the accident to the police reporter.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is report what happened, so the correct answer is "gave a description of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "gave a description of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
A media group has just bought the TV channel. **control**
The TV channel is now ...... the media group.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is owned/managed by, so the correct answer is "under the control of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "under the control of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
You'll be wasting your time if you try to apply for that internship. **point**
There ...... to apply for that internship.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not worth trying, so the correct answer is "is no point in trying".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is no point in trying", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
I believe that social media shapes public opinion. **view**
It ...... that social media shapes public opinion.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is personal opinion, so the correct answer is "is my view".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is my view", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The editor will probably make an announcement tomorrow morning. **likely**
The editor ...... an announcement tomorrow morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is probable future, so the correct answer is "is likely to make".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is likely to make", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
A documentary about climate change will be shown instead of the film tonight. **place**
Tonight, the film, ...... a documentary about climate change will be shown.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is instead of, so the correct answer is "in place of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in place of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The politician wouldn't say anything about the investigation. **comment**
The politician refused ...... the investigation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is would not speak about, so the correct answer is "to comment on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "to comment on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.B: "few" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "much" is correct.

wrongOptions.C: "lots" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "much" is correct.

wrongOptions.many: "many" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "much" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.
